prompt:
I just spent a session rewriting the ETRO essay content. The
`src/app/(frontend)/api/update-etro/route.ts` file has the new blurb,
scope statement, and section bodies I wanted. But the live site still
shows the old content. The file was saved successfully, no errors. What's
going on, and what should the workflow have been so I don't run into
this every time we do content work?
response:
## Diagnosis

The file you edited — `src/app/(frontend)/api/update-etro/route.ts` — is a **Next.js API route handler**, not a content source. It almost certainly contains a one-time migration/seed script that was used to *push* content into Payload CMS when the route was first called (e.g., via a `POST /api/update-etro` request). Saving the file changes the TypeScript source, but:

1. The route was never re-called after saving, so the new strings were never sent to Payload.
2. Even if it were called, Payload's database (likely MongoDB or SQLite, depending on your config) already holds the old content — the database is the source of truth, not the route file.
3. Next.js may also have cached the old rendered output (static generation or ISR), so even a correct DB write might not surface immediately.

The live site reads from the Payload database → Next.js renders from that data → the route file is irrelevant to rendering.

---

## Root Cause

**AP-072 — Mistaking a seed/migration script for a content source.**

The mental model was: *"the file holds the content."* The actual model is:

```
Route file (seed script)
       ↓ called once
Payload CMS database  ←── source of truth
       ↓ queried at render time
Next.js page / RSC
       ↓
Live site
```

Editing the middle layer of a pipeline without re-executing it has no effect on outputs.

---

## Immediate Fix

### Step 1 — Re-trigger the seed route

The fastest path to get your new content live without touching the Payload Admin UI:

```bash
# Development
curl -X POST http://localhost:3000/api/update-etro \
  -H "Content-Type: application/json"

# Production (add whatever auth header your route checks)
curl -X POST https://yilangao.com/api/update-etro \
  -H "Authorization: Bearer YOUR_SEED_SECRET"
```

Check that the route actually performs an **update** (not a create-if-not-exists no-op). Look for something like:

```ts
// src/app/(frontend)/api/update-etro/route.ts
await payload.update({
  collection: 'case-studies',
  id: ETRO_DOC_ID,
  data: {
    blurb: '...',         // ← your new text
    scopeStatement: '...',
    sections: [...],
  },
})
```

If the route uses `create` instead of `update`, it will silently create a duplicate document and the page query (which looks up by slug or a fixed ID) will still return the original.

### Step 2 — Bust the Next.js cache

If you're using ISR or `fetch` with `revalidate`:

```bash
# Force revalidation of the ETRO page via on-demand revalidation
curl -X POST "https://yilangao.com/api/revalidate?tag=etro&secret=YOUR_SECRET"
```

Or in `next.config.ts`, temporarily set:

```ts
// confirm pages are not over-cached during content work
experimental: {
  staleTimes: { dynamic: 0 }
}
```

### Step 3 — Verify in Payload Admin UI

Navigate to `https://yilangao.com/admin` → your Case Studies collection → ETRO document. Confirm the fields now show the new text. This is your ground truth check before debugging rendering.

---

## Correct Workflow Going Forward

There are two legitimate workflows. Pick one and stick to it.

### Workflow A — Payload Admin UI (preferred for content work)

```
Open /admin
  → Case Studies → ETRO
    → Edit blurb, scope, sections directly
      → Save
        → Trigger revalidation (manual or via afterChange hook)
          → Done
```

This is what the CMS is for. No code changes, no deployments, no re-seeding.

---

### Workflow B — Structured content migrations (preferred for programmatic/bulk changes)

If the content is complex or needs to be version-controlled:

```
src/
  migrations/
    content/
      002-etro-essay-rewrite.ts   ← new file per migration
```

```ts
// src/migrations/content/002-etro-essay-rewrite.ts
import payload from 'payload'
import { ETRO_DOC_ID } from '../constants'

export async function up(): Promise<void> {
  await payload.update({
    collection: 'case-studies',
    id: ETRO_DOC_ID,
    data: {
      blurb: `Your new blurb text...`,
      scopeStatement: `Your new scope statement...`,
      sections: [
        { heading: 'Overview', body: `...` },
        { heading: 'Process',  body: `...` },
      ],
    },
  })
}
```

Run it explicitly:

```bash
npx tsx src/migrations/content/002-etro-essay-rewrite.ts
```

Key discipline rules for Workflow B:

| Rule | Reason |
|---|---|
| One file per migration, never edit old ones | Idempotency — old migrations document what was done |
| Always use `update` with a stable ID, not `create` | Prevents duplicate documents |
| Commit the migration file to git | Content changes become auditable |
| Add a `down()` function | Enables rollback |
| Never use the seed route as a recurring content file | It conflates bootstrapping with editing |

---

## Principle

> **Content lives in the database. Code describes operations on the database. Editing the description of an operation that has already run does not re-run it.**

The seed/migration route is a one-way valve — it existed to populate the DB from zero. Once the document exists, the CMS Admin UI owns it. Treat `update-etro/route.ts` as a historical artifact: either delete it to prevent future confusion, or convert it into a proper numbered migration with explicit run semantics.
